section.title: The Beginning of the Gospel
section.sectionRationale: Mark opens not with a birth narrative but with a headline and a countdown. He announces the good news of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, roots it in Isaiah's promise, and races through John's preparatory ministry, the baptism where heaven tears open and the Father names His Son, and the wilderness testing. In thirteen verses the whole Gospel is set in motion.
section.thesis: The good news is a person: Jesus the Christ, the Son of God, whose coming was promised of old, announced by a wilderness herald, sealed by the Father's voice and the Spirit's descent, and immediately proven in the crucible of testing.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the title of the good news; the messenger who prepares the way; the baptism where the Son is anointed and named; and the forty days of testing in the wilderness.

units.label: The title of the good news
units.note: Jesus Christ, the Son of God

verses.text: This is the beginning of the {{euangelion|gospel}} of Jesus {{christos|Christ}}, the Son of God.
As it is written in Isaiah the prophet: “Behold, I will send My messenger ahead of You, who will prepare Your way.”
“A voice of one calling in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the Lord, make straight paths for Him.’”
John the Baptist appeared in the wilderness, preaching a {{baptisma|baptism}} of {{metanoia|repentance}} for the forgiveness of sins.
People went out to him from all of Jerusalem and the countryside of Judea. Confessing their sins, they were baptized by him in the Jordan River.
John was clothed in camel’s hair, with a leather belt around his waist. His food was locusts and wild honey.
And he proclaimed: “After me will come One more powerful than I, the straps of whose sandals I am not worthy to stoop down and untie.
I baptize you with water, but He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.”

units.label: The baptism of Jesus
units.note: the heavens torn open, the Father's voice

verses.text: In those days Jesus came from Nazareth in Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan.
As soon as Jesus came up out of the water, He saw the heavens breaking open and the {{pneuma|Spirit}} descending on Him like a dove.
And a voice came from heaven: “You are My beloved Son; in You I am well pleased.”
At once the Spirit drove Jesus into the wilderness,
and He was there for forty days, being {{peirazo|tempted}} by Satan. He was with the wild animals, and the angels ministered to Him.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, glad tidings, the announcement of a victory or a royal accession. Mark uses it as the title of his whole book and its first word after the heading. The good news is not first a set of ideas but the arrival of a person, Jesus, and the saving reign of God that comes with Him.

christos.gloss: Anointed One, the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew Messiah. It marks Jesus as the promised King of David's line, anointed by God to rule and to rescue. Mark's Gospel will slowly unveil what kind of Messiah He is, one who reigns from a cross.

baptisma.gloss: A washing or immersion. John's baptism is a public act of turning, an outward sign of confessed sin and repentance, preparing people for the greater One who baptizes not merely with water but with the Holy Spirit.

metanoia.gloss: A change of mind that turns the whole life around; repentance. It is more than regret; it is a reorientation away from sin and toward God. John's baptism of repentance readies the people to receive the coming King.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. The Holy Spirit descends on Jesus at His baptism, anointing Him for His mission, and then drives Him into the wilderness. The same Spirit He will pour out marks the age of the good news.

peirazo.gloss: To test, try, or tempt. In the wilderness Jesus is tested by Satan, standing where Israel fell and where Adam fell, the faithful Son who does not break under trial. The verb runs through the Gospel as opponents keep testing Him.

basic.context: Mark starts his Gospel at a sprint. There is no manger, no shepherds, just a headline: this is the good news of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. He shows that it all fulfils Isaiah's promise of a messenger who prepares the way. John the Baptist is that messenger, calling people to repent and be baptized. Then Jesus Himself is baptized, the heavens tear open, the Spirit comes down, and the Father's voice names Him beloved Son. At once the Spirit sends Him into the wilderness to be tested for forty days.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as Mark's overture, sounding every theme he will develop: Jesus as the promised Christ and Son of God, the coming of God's kingdom, the Spirit's power, and the shadow of testing and conflict. The key question is not what Jesus teaches yet but who He is. Mark answers in the first line and then lets the Father confirm it from heaven.
basic.originalLanguages: The very first word after the heading is euangelion, good news. Jesus is the Christos, the anointed King. John preaches a baptisma, a baptism, of metanoia, repentance, a turning of the whole life. At the Jordan the pneuma, the Spirit, descends, and in the wilderness Jesus is peirazo, tested, by Satan.
basic.typology: The forty days of testing echo Israel's forty years in the wilderness and Moses' forty days on the mountain. Where Israel grumbled and fell, Jesus stands firm. He is the true and faithful Son doing what the first son, Adam, and the son Israel failed to do.
basic.christ: The whole passage is about Christ. He is named the Son of God in verse 1 and confirmed as beloved Son by the Father's voice in verse 11. The mightier One John promised, who baptizes with the Spirit, is Jesus. He is the Lord whose way Isaiah said would be prepared.
basic.god: God speaks and acts here as Father, Son, and Spirit together: the Father's voice from heaven, the Son coming up from the water, the Spirit descending like a dove. God keeps His ancient promise, sends the herald, and openly delights in His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 40 and Malachi 3 promise the messenger and the prepared way. Exodus 4 calls Israel God's son; Jesus is the true Son. Matthew 4 and Luke 4 give the fuller account of the temptation.
basic.oneStory: Centuries of promise now come to a head. The Lord Himself is coming, His way prepared by a wilderness herald, and He arrives as Jesus, the anointed Son. The story that began with a fall in a garden now finds its rescuer standing firm in a wilderness, and the good news is under way.

context.literary: These verses are Mark's prologue, the reader's privileged briefing. We are told plainly who Jesus is, the Christ and Son of God, before any human character in the story knows it. Mark writes fast, with his signature immediately, and stacks scenes back to back. The Father's declaration at the baptism forms an inclusio with the centurion's confession at the cross, Mark 15:39.
context.historical: John the Baptist appears in the Judean wilderness dressed like Elijah, drawing crowds from Jerusalem and Judea to the Jordan. First century Jews longed for the promised messenger and the coming of God's reign after long silence. Baptism as a public act of confession and turning prepared people to meet the One greater than John.
context.canonical: Mark opens the New Testament's earliest Gospel by binding it to the prophets: the way of the Lord, the messenger, the Spirit-anointed Servant of Isaiah. The tearing of the heavens and the Father's words draw on Psalm 2 and Isaiah 42, presenting Jesus as royal Son and chosen Servant in one.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce from the first line that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, to show His coming as the fulfilment of prophecy, and to establish His identity by the Father's own voice and the Spirit's anointing before the ministry unfolds.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes unique, unrepeatable events, the baptism and anointing of the Son and His testing. It is not prescribing a pattern of ministry to copy step by step, though it does teach the abiding call to repent and believe and the reality of testing in the faithful life.

debates.question: Why is Jesus, who is sinless, baptized with a baptism of repentance?

views.label: Identifying with sinners
views.case: Jesus is not confessing His own sin but stepping into the place of the people He came to save, standing with sinners in the Jordan and beginning the path that leads to the cross where He bears their sin.

views.label: Fulfilling all righteousness and inaugurating His mission
views.case: The baptism is His public commissioning: the Spirit anoints Him and the Father names Him, marking the formal start of the Servant's obedient work, doing everything righteousness requires.

debates.bearing: Either way the baptism is not a confession of personal guilt but the sinless Son taking His place with sinners and being anointed for the rescue only He can accomplish.

debates.question: What does it mean that the Spirit drove Jesus into the wilderness to be tested?

views.label: A necessary proving of the faithful Son
views.case: The same Spirit who anointed Him leads Him straight into conflict; sonship is proven, not exempted from testing, and Jesus succeeds where Israel and Adam failed.

views.label: The opening skirmish of a longer war
views.case: The wilderness testing is the first clash in the Gospel's ongoing conflict with Satan, whom Jesus will keep defeating through exorcisms and finally at the cross and empty tomb.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold that testing is not a sign of the Father's displeasure; the beloved Son is led into battle and stands firm, the strong One who can bind the strong man.

study.originalLanguages: Mark's opening word is euangelion, good news, and its content is a person, Jesus the Christos, the anointed King and Son of God. John's ministry is a baptisma of metanoia, a baptism of repentance, a turning of the whole life toward God. At the Jordan the pneuma, the Spirit, descends on Jesus like a dove, anointing Him, and the same Spirit at once drives Him into the wilderness where He is peirazo, tested, by Satan. The vocabulary sets the stage: the Spirit-anointed King has come, and the war for the world's rescue has begun.

typology.type: The messenger who prepares the way
typology.fulfillment: John the Baptist as the promised Elijah, Malachi 3:1 and 4:5, preparing the Lord's coming, Matthew 11:14.
Jesus, the true Israel and last Adam, standing firm where Israel's forty years and Adam's testing ended in failure, Deuteronomy 8:2 and Romans 5:19.
Jesus as the royal Son of Psalm 2 and the chosen Servant of Isaiah 42:1, anointed by the Spirit for His mission.

study.christ: This passage is entirely occupied with the identity of Christ. Mark names Him in the first verse as Jesus Christ, the Son of God, and the Father confirms it from heaven: You are My beloved Son. He is the Lord whose way Isaiah promised, the mightier One John heralded, the Servant on whom the Spirit rests, and the faithful Son who withstands Satan's testing. Everything that follows in Mark, His authority, His suffering, and His resurrection, flows from who He is announced to be here.
study.god: God is revealed as Father, Son, and Spirit acting in perfect harmony at the Jordan: the Spirit descends, the Son rises from the water, the Father speaks His delight. God keeps His long promise, sends the herald exactly as foretold, tears open heaven to draw near, and openly declares His love for His Son before a single miracle is done, grounding the Son's mission in the Father's pleasure rather than in any achievement yet performed.

crossReferences.note: The voice calling in the wilderness to prepare the way of the Lord, fulfilled in John.
God's promise to send His messenger ahead to prepare the way, applied to John the Baptist.
You are My Son, the royal decree echoed in the Father's voice at the baptism.
The chosen Servant on whom God puts His Spirit, in whom His soul delights.

study.oneStory: The long-promised day has come. After centuries of prophecy and silence, the Lord Himself draws near, His way prepared by a wilderness herald just as Isaiah said. He arrives as Jesus, the anointed Son, anointed by the Spirit and named by the Father. Where Adam fell and Israel failed, this Son stands firm under testing. The good news the whole Bible pointed toward is finally under way, and the rest of Mark races to show what this King has come to do.

quiz.q: What is the good news, according to Mark's opening line?

options.text: A person: Jesus Christ, the Son of God.
options.note: Mark 1:1 announces the gospel as the arrival of Jesus, the Christ and Son of God.

options.text: A message about the coming kingdom of God.
options.note: True and closely related, but Mark's headline centres the good news first on the person of Jesus.

options.text: A new set of moral teachings from a great rabbi.
options.note: Jesus does teach, but Mark presents Him as far more than a teacher; the good news is who He is.

options.text: A political program to free Israel from Rome.
options.note: Mark's gospel is about God's saving reign in Christ, not a nationalist revolt.

quiz.q: How does John the Baptist describe the One coming after him?

options.text: More powerful than John, whose sandals John is unworthy to untie, and who baptizes with the Holy Spirit.
options.note: Mark 1:7-8 gives John's exact words about the mightier One.

options.text: A prophet like John who will also baptize people in the Jordan.
options.note: He does baptize, but John stresses the coming One is far greater and baptizes with the Spirit, not merely water.

options.text: A teacher who will confirm John's own authority.
options.note: John points away from himself to a vastly greater One, not to bolster his own standing.

options.text: A military leader who will overthrow Rome.
options.note: John announces the Spirit-baptizing Lord, not a military deliverer.

options.text: The heavens break open, the Spirit descends like a dove, and the Father calls Him His beloved Son.
options.note: Mark 1:10-11 records all three: the opened heavens, the Spirit, and the Father's voice.

options.text: The Spirit descends on Jesus to anoint Him.
options.note: True, but partial; the fuller answer includes the torn heavens and the Father's declaration.

options.text: John recognizes Jesus and declares Him the Messiah to the crowd.
options.note: The confirmation here comes from heaven, the Father's voice, not from John announcing it to the crowd.

options.text: Nothing unusual happens; it is an ordinary baptism.
options.note: Mark describes an extraordinary Trinitarian moment, not an ordinary one.

quiz.q: Why does the Father's declaration of delight come before Jesus has done any public work?

options.text: The Son is loved and approved for who He is, before and apart from any ministry achievement.
options.note: The Father delights in the Son at the outset, grounding the mission in love, not in performance.

options.text: Because Jesus had already lived a sinless life in Nazareth.
options.note: His prior obedience is real, but the point of the heavenly voice is the Father's settled love for His Son, not a reward earned.

options.text: Because the crowd needed to be convinced Jesus was worthy.
options.note: The declaration is the Father's own delight, not a public argument staged for the crowd.

options.text: Because Jesus had earned it by being baptized.
options.note: The Father's love is not a wage paid for baptism; it precedes and undergirds the whole mission.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Jesus being tested in the wilderness for forty days?

options.text: He stands firm as the faithful Son where Israel and Adam failed under testing.
options.note: The forty days echo Israel's wilderness years; Jesus succeeds where they fell.

options.text: It shows testing is a normal part of the life the Spirit leads.
options.note: True, the same Spirit leads Him into testing, but the deeper point is His victorious faithfulness as the true Son.

options.text: It proves Jesus was uncertain about His mission.
options.note: The testing proves His steadfastness, not uncertainty.

options.text: It means Satan is stronger than Jesus.
options.note: Jesus withstands Satan; He is the strong One who will bind the strong man.

questions: Mark calls the arrival of Jesus good news. In what areas of your life do you most need to hear that the gospel is first a person to trust rather than a task to perform?
John called people to repentance, a turning of the whole life. Where is God calling you to turn, honestly and concretely, toward Him this week?
The Father declared His delight in the Son before any public work. How would resting in God's love, rather than trying to earn it, change the way you serve Him?
The Spirit led Jesus straight into testing. How does it steady you to know that trials can come to those God loves and are not a sign of His displeasure?
Jesus stood firm where Adam and Israel fell. How does His faithfulness in the wilderness give you hope when you face temptation?

translationNotes.note: Some early manuscripts read simply the beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, without the Son of God, but the strongest witnesses and Mark's whole design support the fuller reading, which frames the entire book.
Mark attributes the combined quotation to Isaiah though verse 2 draws on Malachi 3:1 and verse 3 on Isaiah 40:3; naming the major prophet for a blended citation was a common ancient practice.
The heavens breaking open renders a vivid Greek word for tearing; the same verb describes the temple curtain torn at Jesus' death, Mark 15:38, framing His ministry from open heaven to opened access.

One em dash in the surrounding chapter was outside this section. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
